quoteFirst: wind blowing, which prevented our hearing any firing, and we could see very little shelling. Our air plane first reported that a certain fort, which stood about a mile in advance of the enemy's left flank, was strongly held; but we seem to have shelled them out of that pretty easily, for
quoteLast: it reported again that the enemy had left his trenches on the Canal.
title:
Letters from Mesopotamia in 1915 and January, 1916
From Robert Palmer, who was killed in the Battle of Um El Hannah, June 21, 1916, aged 27 years
author: Palmer, Robert Stafford Arthur, 1888-1916

quoteLast: , a rebel sharp-shooter (whom, from his dress, I took to be a Carolinian) undertook to crawl up to the top of their earth-works, behind a stump that hid him from the view of our sharp-shooters, that he might be enabled to get a shot at them. He crawled along, with his gun in his right hand, till near the spot, and then took off his big-brimmed hat and turned his head up sideways to look around the side of the stump at our sharp-shooters.
title:
Four Years a Scout and Spy
"General Bunker", One of Lieut. General Grant's Most Daring and Successful Scouts, Being a Narrative of ... the Experience of Corporal Ruggles During Four Years' Service as a Scout and Spy for the Federal Army
author: Downs, E. C. (Edward C.)

quoteLast: and mounting the horses, already waiting, dashed away in the direction the robbers had taken. With him, Captain Bill had Rangers McCauley, Harwell, Sullivan, Queen, and McClure--the tried, picked men whom Lewis and Crawford had been most anxious to avoid.
title: Captain Bill McDonald, Texas Ranger: A Story of Frontier Reform
author: Paine, Albert Bigelow, 1861-1937; Roosevelt, Theodore, 1858-1919 [Contributor]

quoteFirst: Leaving these in the custody of the St. Armand volunteers, the column of attack started for Pigeon Hill
quoteLast: The Granby and Waterloo volunteers, commanded by Captain Millar, formed the advance guard, being followed by two 12-pounder Armstrong guns of Captain Balfour’s battery, with their complements of artillerymen, commanded by Captain Phipps, R.A.
title: The battles of the world
author: Borthwick, J. Douglas (John Douglas), 1831-1912

quoteLast: the Battalion moved on to Houplines, and were still under orders to advance. At 6.30 p.m. we arrived at Templemars, remaining there for the night. Our reception here was even more cordial than before. Battalion Headquarters were established at the Mayor’s house.
title: The Fourth Battalion, The King's Own (Royal Lancaster Regiment) and the Great War
author: Wadham, W. F. A. (Walter F. A.); Crossley, J.

quoteFirst: Temperature, in summer, commences its diurnal elevation about 4 A.M., also, and rises till
quoteLast: From that time it falls with very little variation till 4 o'clock the next morning. It has but one maximum and one minimum in the twenty-four hours.
title: The Philosophy of the Weather. And a Guide to Its Changes
author: Butler, T. B. (Thomas Belden), 1806-1873

quoteFirst: "About 3 P.M. all communications with the river (landing) ceased, and it became evident to me that the enemy was turning the right and left flanks of our army. . . .
quoteLast: the whole Union right, comprising the Forty-sixth Ohio, which had held that flank two hours or more, was driven back in disorder, and the Confederate flanking force cut the center off from the landing, as stated by Colonel Geddes, soon after General Johnston's fall.
title: The Rise and Fall of the Confederate Government, Volume 2
author: Davis, Jefferson, 1808-1889

quoteLast: I brought up under a solitary tree, in consequence of heavy rain: this was upon the left bank. In the afternoon, however, we again pushed forward, and soon lost sight of every other object amidst reeds of great height.
title: Two Expeditions into the Interior of Southern Australia — Volume I
author: Sturt, Charles, 1795-1869

quoteLast: , Scott found himself entering an ancient and dilapidated looking block in a rather disreputable part of the city.  He had fulfilled his appointment with Mr. Sutherland, and after an hour's conversation both gentlemen appeared very sanguine regarding the case under consideration.
title: That Mainwaring Affair
author: Barbour, A. Maynard (Anna Maynard), -1941

quoteFirst: The ammunition would have arrived with De Wet. That is circumstantial evidence; but last night
quoteLast: I got the following from the cable-cart. It is from our friend the De Wet expert, dated last night from Orange River Station (_takes out paper and reads_): 'Despatches captured ordering concentration of all available commandoes at Strydenburg to meet De Wet on the evening of the 26th'--that is to-night, sir.
title: On the Heels of De Wet
author: James, Lionel, 1871-1955
